title: Public Records Request: Clearview

communications.subject: Louisiana Public Records Law Request: Public Records Request: Clearview
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Louisiana Public Records Law, I hereby request the following records:

- All contracts, licenses, MOUs, MOAs, data agreements, letters of intent, State of Work documents, Scope of Work documents, and other agreement documents with Clearview AI, Inc. that were active any time between 1/1/2021 and the date this request is fulfilled.
- All financial documents or records of financial transactions, exchanges or obligations related to Clearview AI, Inc. Responsive materials include all invoices, bills, receipts, check stubs, or any other records, including those possessed in an electronic format.
- A copy of all training materials, including but not limited to any handouts, workbooks, assessments, presentations, slides, videos, audio files, or other materials, either developed in-house or provided to the agency pertaining to the agency's use of Clearview AI, Inc. products.
- A copy of all agency manuals either developed in-house or provided to the agency pertaining to the agency's use of Clearview AI, Inc. products.
- A copy of all agency policies and procedures either developed in-house or provided to the agency pertaining to the agency's use of Clearview AI, Inc. products.
- A roster of all agency employees who either had or currently have a user account for Clearview AI, Inc. products or had or currently have internal agency permissions to use Clearview AI, Inc. products any time between 1/1/2024 and the date this request is fulfilled.
- A roster of all agency employees who have completed agency training for the use of Clearview AI, Inc. products any time between 1/1/2024 and the date this request is fulfilled.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 3 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

e

title: AI Chatbot Interaction Logs — Executive Office for United States Attorneys

communications.subject: Freedom of Information Act Request: AI Chatbot Interaction Logs — Executive Office for United States Attorneys
communications.communication:
Subject: FOIA Request — AI Chatbot Interaction Logs

April 7, 2026

Office of Information Policy
U.S. Department of Justice
441 G Street NW, 6th Floor
Washington, DC 20530
DOJ.OIP.FOIA@usdoj.gov

Dear FOIA Officer,

I am writing on behalf of a team of researchers and journalists from Stanford University, the University of Southern California, KNKX Public Radio and the New York Times. We are studying how government agencies are adopting and using AI tools in their daily work.

We are writing to request records related to the Department of Justice's use of generative AI tools by department employees.

We are requesting the following records:

1. All interaction logs from the OIG internal Azure OpenAI chatbot (DOJ-0195), DEA policy query chatbot (DOJ-0316), FBI policy chatbot (DOJ-0269), ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, Claude, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by DOJ headquarters employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from January 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing DOJ employee use of generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.


We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

DOJ's 2025 AI Use Case Inventory documents 315 AI use cases — up from 241 in 2024 and 15 in 2023 — including multiple component-level GenAI chatbots across OIG, DEA, FBI, EOUSA, and BOP (downloadable at https://www.justice.gov/ai/ai-inventory; see also FedScoop, Feb 11, 2026: https://fedscoop.com/justice-department-artificial-intelligence-ai-surveillance-inventory-predictive-technology-algorithm-bias/). We understand the other agencies under DOJ use chatbots: the Bureau of Prisons has used ChatGPT to "improve the quality of written communication, emails, and fliers" and department-wide Microsoft Copilot deployment is also underway (https://www.justice.gov/media/1426076/dl).

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address or phone number below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data, which generates a downloadable ZIP file containing conversations in HTML format. For Claude, export is similar, except via "Settings > Privacy > Export Data". For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console, the Purview compliance center, or individual user account settings. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Under the Federal Records Act, recorded information created or received by a federal agency in connection with public business can qualify as a federal record when it is preserved or appropriate for preservation. 44 U.S.C. § 3301(a)(1)(A). NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely federal records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02. Consistent with that approach, the Army's August 26, 2025 AI compliance memorandum requires Army system owners to capture and manage AI prompts and AI-generated content and to maintain record material needed to support FOIA responses. See "Army CIO Issues Guidance on AI Compliance With Records Management, FOIA," ExecutiveGov (Sept. 2, 2025), https://www.executivegov.com/articles/army-garciga-application-owners-ai-records-mgmt.

All interaction logs from the OIG internal Azure OpenAI chatbot (DOJ-0195), DEA policy query chatbot (DOJ-0316), FBI policy chatbot (DOJ-0269), ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini, Claude, or any other generative AI chatbot tool — including prompts submitted by DOJ headquarters employees and responses generated by the AI — for the period from January 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed. We request these records in their native electronic format where available (e.g., HTML exports, JSON, or CSV).

2. Any policies, guidelines, acceptable use policies, or training materials governing DOJ employee use of generative AI chatbot tools.

3. Any data retention or deletion policies applicable to AI chatbot interaction logs, including any schedules for automatic deletion of interaction data.


We are seeking the substantive content of AI interactions for research purposes. We do not need personally identifying information of individual employees (such as names and email addresses).

DOJ's 2025 AI Use Case Inventory documents 315 AI use cases — up from 241 in 2024 and 15 in 2023 — including multiple component-level GenAI chatbots across OIG, DEA, FBI, EOUSA, and BOP (downloadable at https://www.justice.gov/ai/ai-inventory; see also FedScoop, Feb 11, 2026: https://fedscoop.com/justice-department-artificial-intelligence-ai-surveillance-inventory-predictive-technology-algorithm-bias/).  We understand that other agencies under DOJ use chatbots as well: the Bureau of Prisons has used ChatGPT to "improve the quality of written communication, emails, and fliers" (https://www.justice.gov/media/1426076/dl) and a department-wide Microsoft Copilot deployment is also underway. 

We note that records of this type have been made available in response to public records requests in other jurisdictions. See Cascade PBS, WA City Officials Are Using ChatGPT to Write Government Documents (Aug. 2025), https://www.cascadepbs.org/news/2025/08/wa-city-officials-are-using-chatgpt-to-write-government-documents/; see also Texas State AI Usage — Texas Department of Transportation (TxDOT), MuckRock, https://www.muckrock.com/foi/texas-109/texas-state-ai-usage-texas-department-of-transportation-txdot-196227/#files.

The results of this research will be published in peer-reviewed academic venues and will contribute to public understanding of how public servants interact with AI systems. This request is made by an academic research institution for scholarly, non-commercial purposes; as such, we request a fee waiver or reduction. If a fee waiver is not granted, please inform me of estimated costs before proceeding. I am willing to pay fees up to $20.

I understand that this may be a novel type of records request and I am happy to answer any technical questions. Please feel free to contact me at the email address or phone number below.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

Sincerely,
Alexander Spangher
Postdoctoral Researcher
Stanford University
alexspan@stanford.edu

P.S. To facilitate your response, we note that ChatGPT conversation histories can be exported by individual users via Settings > Data Controls > Export Data, which generates a downloadable ZIP file containing conversations in HTML format. For Claude, export is similar, except via "Settings > Privacy > Export Data". For Microsoft Copilot, interaction histories may be available through the Microsoft 365 admin console, the Purview compliance center, or individual user account settings. We are happy to provide additional technical guidance if that would be helpful.

P.P.S. Under the Federal Records Act, recorded information created or received by a federal agency in connection with public business can qualify as a federal record when it is preserved or appropriate for preservation. 44 U.S.C. § 3301(a)(1)(A). NARA has made clear that chats and other electronic messages used for agency business are likely federal records. NARA Bulletin 2023-02 (Jan. 5, 2023), https://www.archives.gov/records-mgmt/bulletins/2023/2023-02. Consistent with that approach, the Army's August 26, 2025 AI compliance memorandum requires Army system owners to capture and manage AI prompts and AI-generated content and to maintain record material needed to support FOIA responses. See "Army CIO Issues Guidance on AI Compliance With Records Management, FOIA," ExecutiveGov (Sept. 2, 2025), https://www.executivegov.com/articles/army-garciga-application-owners-ai-records-mgmt.

title: Faculty Search Records Request

communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Illinois Freedom of Information Act (5 ILCS 140), I hereby request the following public records related to the faculty recruitment and hiring process for the 2026-2027 (current) academic cycle within the Grainger College of Engineering.

This request includes records for tenure-track, non-tenure track, teaching, and Specialized Faculty positions. The specific list of job openings/requisition ID include, but are not limited to:
1. Department of Aerospace Engineering - Open Rank Faculty Search / Requisition ID 1032680 Posted on 10/8/2025
2. Department of Industrial & Enterprise Systems Engineering- Open Rank Faculty Search / Requisition ID 1032898 Posted on 10/13/2025
3. Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering – Open Rank Faculty Search / Requisition ID 1033201 Posted on 11/11/2025
4. Department of Bioengineering - Teaching Faculty (Open Rank) / Requisition ID 1034104 Posted on 12/19/2025
5. The Grainger College of Engineering: Professor - Siebel School of Computing and Data Science / Requisition ID 1035082 Posted on 3/20/2026

Requested Records:

Please note that the request is for any and all interviews which may have occurred between January 1, 2026 and April 25, 2026.

1. Interview Recordings: Copies of any and all video or audio recordings of candidate screening interviews, specifically for candidates who provided written consent for such recordings to be made and maintained, as outlined in the UIUC Recruitment and Hiring Guidelines (Effective January 1, 2026).

2. Faculty Candidate Seminar recordings: Copies of any and all video or audio recordings of faculty candidate seminars, specifically for candidates who provided written consent for such recordings to be made and maintained, as outlined in the UIUC Recruitment and Hiring Guidelines (Effective January 1, 2026), even if the Faculty Candidate Seminar is publically advertised as a Departmental Seminar due to confidentiality requests from the candidate.

3. Interview Notes: Copies of all formal interview notes taken by search committee members (excluding personal "memory aides" not shared with the committee, as defined by Illinois law).

This request specifically EXCLUDES:

1. Evaluation Documents: Search committee evaluation forms, rubrics, ranking sheets, and final reports regarding the candidates for the specified positions.
2. Correspondence: Emails, memoranda, and internal communications regarding the selection, ranking, and final recommendations for these hires.
3. Meeting minutes of the Faculty Hiring Committee.


Privacy and Redactions:

Consistent with Section 7(1)(b) and 7(1)(c) of the Act, I request that you redact sensitive personal information such as Social Security Numbers (SSN), home addresses, personal phone numbers, and any other "unique identifiers" as defined by FOIA.

If you determine that any specific record or portion of a record is exempt from disclosure (e.g., the names of unsuccessful candidates who did not consent), please provide a written justification for each redaction or denial, citing the specific FOIA exemption, and release the remaining non-exempt portions of the records.

Format of Records:
I request that these records be provided in their original electronic format (e.g., PDF, MP4, or Excel) via email or a secure file-sharing link.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within the five (5) business days required by the Act.

Sincerely,

Joseph Sentien Sief

911 Audio

Body-Worn Camera – Initial Response
Body-worn camera video and audio from the first responding officer(s) at County Road 1 near County Road 102, documenting arrival, initial scene assessment, and confirmation of deceased victim(s), including Irma Miller and Wilma Miller.

Body-worn camera video and audio capturing the initial statement provided by Sarah Peterson at approximately 8:30 AM – 9:30 AM on September 30, 2023, at the crash scene, including the identification of herself as the driver of the Toyota 4Runner.

Body-Worn Camera – Evidence Collection
Body-worn camera video and audio documenting evidence collection at the scene on September 30, 2023, including documentation of the Amish buggy wreckage, Toyota 4Runner damage, roadway debris, and the recovered items or suspected contraband.

Crime Scene Photographs
Crime scene photographs taken at County Road 1 and County Road 102 depict the impact point, final resting positions of the Amish buggy and Toyota 4Runner, and the deceased victims as documented at the scene.

the digital evidence images, such as screenshots or photographs of text messages recovered from suspect mobile devices

Ch. 13), I respectfully request access to the following multimedia records related to a fatal motor vehicle collision involving an Amish buggy and a Toyota 4Runner that occurred on September 30, 2023, at approximately 8:25 AM – 9:30 AM at County Road 1 near County Road 102, Sumner Township, Fillmore County, Minnesota, resulting in the deaths of Irma Miller and Wilma Miller.

Example Requested Records:

911 Audio
911 audio of the call(s) placed by passersby or involved parties on September 30, 2023, at approximately 8:25 AM – 8:45 AM, reporting a motor vehicle collision involving an Amish buggy at County Road 1 near County Road 102, Fillmore County, Minnesota. (Caller name unknown)
Body-Worn Camera – Initial Response
Body-worn camera video and audio from the first responding officer(s) at County Road 1 near County Road 102, documenting arrival, initial scene assessment, and confirmation of deceased victim(s), including Irma Miller and Wilma Miller.

Body-Worn Camera – Specific Event (Witness Statement)
Body-worn camera video and audio capturing the initial statement provided by Sarah Peterson at approximately 8:30 AM – 9:30 AM on September 30, 2023, at the crash scene, including the identification of herself as the driver of the Toyota 4Runner.

In-Car Dash Camera
In-car dash camera video and audio capturing the arrival, positioning, and on-scene activity of Fillmore County Sheriff’s Office vehicles at approximately 8:25 AM on September 30, 2023, at County Road 1 near County Road 102, including the recorded interactions at the scene.

Body-Worn Camera – Evidence Collection
Body-worn camera video and audio documenting evidence collection at the scene on September 30, 2023, including documentation of the Amish buggy wreckage, Toyota 4Runner damage, roadway debris, and the recovered items or suspected contraband.

Crime Scene Photographs
Crime scene photographs taken at County Road 1 and County Road 102 depict the impact point, final resting positions of the Amish buggy and Toyota 4Runner, and the deceased victims as documented at the scene.

Evidence Processing Photographs
Evidence processing photographs associated with Case Nos. 23-CR-24-106 and 23-CR-24-107, specifically including:
Damage to the Toyota 4Runner
The destroyed Amish buggy
the digital evidence images, such as screenshots or photographs of text messages recovered from suspect mobile devices

Body-Worn Camera / Dash Camera – Arrest or Post-Investigation Contact
Body-worn camera/ dashcam/MVR video and audio documenting the arrest, charging contact, or surrender/processing of Samantha Jo Peterson and Sarah Beth Peterson following the investigation (approximately February 2024), including the custody transfer or booking-related footage if available.

Interrogation Room Recordings
Full interview room video and audio recording(s) of the custodial or non-custodial interviews of Samantha Jo Peterson and Sarah Beth Peterson conducted by the Fillmore County Sheriff’s Office or the Minnesota Bureau of Criminal Apprehension between September 30, 2023, and February 2024.

Witness Interviews – Rochester Hy-Vee Employees
Full video and audio recording(s) of witness interviews conducted with coworkers at the Rochester Hy-Vee by the Fillmore County Sheriff’s Office following the incident, specifically regarding the statements made by the suspects about the crash or related events.

CCTV / surveillance video from Hy-Vee (Rochester, MN) for the period 7:00 AM – 10:00 AM on September 30, 2023, depicting the Peterson twins' arrival/departure or the interactions with coworkers immediately following the crash.

Incident Details:

Incident Numbers: 23-CR-24-106 and 23-CR-24-107
Victim(s): Irma Miller, Wilma Miller
Subject(s): Sarah Peterson, Samantha Jo Peterson, Sarah Beth Peterson 
Date of Incident: September 30, 2023
Time of Incident: Approximately 8:25 AM – 9:30 AM
Location of Incident: County Road 1 near County Road 102, Sumner Township, Fillmore County, Minnesota

This request seeks native digital copies of the above-listed multimedia records as maintained by your agency. I am not requesting written reports, CAD logs, transcripts, or administrative summaries at this time.

If any portion of this request is denied, please cite the specific statutory exemption for each denial and release all reasonably segregable portions of responsive records.

Please provide an itemized cost estimate prior to processing. I prefer electronic delivery via secure download link or USB drive.

If your agency does not maintain any portion of these records, please forward this request to the appropriate custodian or advise me accordingly.

Thank you for your time and assistance.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 business days.

Sincerely,

Danielle D.

communications.subject: Iowa Open Records Law Request: Flock Email Requests (Des Moines Police Department)
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Iowa Open Records Law, I hereby request the following records:

Please provide all records of email communications, including attachments and calendar invitations, that were sent to or received from the domain “@flocksafety.com,” between November 1, 2025 and the date this request is fulfilled.

This request includes:
- Emails where @flocksafety.com appears in the sender, recipient (To, CC, BCC), or within the body of the message
- Attachments of any file type, including but not limited to PDFs, presentations, spreadsheets, and images
- Calendar invitations, meeting requests, and related responses involving Flock Safety personnel

Please search all agency-controlled email systems and accounts reasonably likely to contain responsive records, including individual officer accounts, group mailboxes, and archived or backup systems. Records should be provided in native electronic format where possible (e.g., .pst, .msg, or .eml with metadata preserved), including all available metadata fields such as sender, recipients, timestamps, and subject lines.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Alan J. Segal
